---
title: "Security upgrades on my Node.js, npm and Azurite"
date: 2026-05-19
description: "A Fireship video sent me down a rabbit hole — turns out my Node.js had CVEs and my Azurite was quietly sitting on vulnerable dependencies."
image: ../../images/blog/teddy_bear_it_rotten_roots.webp
image-alt: "Teddy bear typing code with rotten roots"
categories: [npm, node.js, azurite, security-update, windows]
draft: false
---

# Supply chain attacks

A few days back [Fireship](https://www.youtube.com/@Fireship) dropped ["A single PR just hijacked the NPM registry…"](https://youtu.be/gwTQLZSIlsU?si=tuvB6pf2RX3Wq29m) - as always, educational and alarming in equal measures.

I'd already been keeping an eye on supply chain risks after the [xz/liblzma near-disaster](https://youtu.be/aoag03mSuXQ?si=XaY2rYktkrwHZ34_) that almost took down the internet.

And the numbers back up the paranoia: Sonatype reports that ["over 99% of open source malware occurred on npm"](https://www.sonatype.com/state-of-the-software-supply-chain/2026/open-source-malware).

So naturally I started wondering: what about my machine?

# How Node.js ended up on my machine 
I'm not a heavy Node.js user — it landed on my Windows machine in November 2024 mostly as collateral from installing other tooling. The last time I consciously updated it was February 2026; npm got a bump in March.

The main reason it's there at all: I run [Azurite](https://github.com/Azure/Azurite) locally to emulate Azure Blob Storage alongside Python Azure Functions tests. Azurite is used alongside Python Azure Functions and generates network traffic, so it's a bit sensitive and needs to be kept in check.

I also had no idea how many other Node.js packages were lurking on my system. Spoiler: a few.


# Trusting but checking
I trust Microsoft's team to keep Azurite maintained. But trust-and-forget is not a security posture just a light edge on hope.

So let's actually check:

- my local npm, node.js, and Azurite security status, and update them if needed  
- the node.js packages on my system and run security checks to detect vulnerabilities
- fix security vulnerabilities

# Updating Node.js and npm

## What I was running
```dosbat
C:\>npm -v
11.14.1

C:\>node -v
v24.14.0
```

Findings:

- **OK.** npm was already on the latest version  
- **Not OK.** Node.js was not latest → [v24.14.1](https://nodejs.org/en/download/archive/v24.14.1) and [v24.15.0](https://nodejs.org/en/download/archive/v24.15.0) were both available  
- **OK.** npm was ahead of the Node.js‑bundled npm (e.g. v11.12.1 in [Node v24.15.0](https://nodejs.org/en/download/archive/v24.15.0)) — a newer patch/minor than what ships with node.js v24.14 or v24.15

## Risk of node.js v24.14.0 vs. LTS v24.15.0 ?

[v24.14.1](https://nodejs.org/en/blog/release/v24.14.1) is a proper security release — 2 high CVEs, 4 medium, 2 low. That alone was enough to act.

[v24.15.0](https://nodejs.org/en/blog/release/v24.15.0) looked even better: per Perplexity (May 18, 2026), it's "mainly a hardening release — low-level fixes in crypto, http, tls, stream, zlib, plus refreshed bundled deps including npm and root certificates." No breaking changes reported, so it became the target.

## Why was node.js outdated?

I installed Node.js from the .msi installer (Cf. default node.exe path below). Turns out the `.msi` installer from nodejs.org (rightfully) has no auto-update mechanism. No reminder, no update.
```dosbat
C:\>where node
C:\Program Files\nodejs\node.exe
```

## Updating node.js
Installing additional tools (nvm-windows, Chocolatey...) to manage the node.js update is out of scope for this post so I'll just run the node-v24.15.0-x64.msi from nodejs.org.

**After installation:**

```dosbat
C:\>node -v
v24.15.0
```

# What's actually installed?

## Global packages

```dosbat
C:\>npm list -g
C:\Users\xxx\AppData\Roaming\npm
+-- azurite@3.35.0
`-- npm@11.14.1

C:\>npm list --parseable -g --depth=0
C:\Users\xxx\AppData\Roaming\npm
C:\Users\xxx\AppData\Roaming\npm\node_modules\azurite
C:\Users\xxx\AppData\Roaming\npm\node_modules\npm
```

I only had one global package: Azurite. Good enough for now.

## Local packages

This [PowerShell script](find-node-projects.qmd) is used to find all node.js projects on my machine.

Output:
```powershell
PS C:\> C:\...\find-node-projects.ps1
=== Scanning for Node.js projects ===
Root path: C:\...\projects

Total Node.js projects found: 0
```

No local packages of node.js projects found.

# Auditing Azurite

Checking Azurite for known vulnerabilities is unlikely to uncover serious threats — it's a maintained Microsoft package and I'm not a security researcher. But version freshness and known CVEs in its dependency tree are worth a look.

## Is Azurite up to date?
```dosbat
C:\>azurite --version
3.35.0
```
It's the latest released version of Azurite on [GitHub releases](https://github.com/Azure/Azurite/releases), so it's up to date.

## Compatibility check
```dosbat
C:\>npm view azurite engines
{ node: '>=10.0.0', vscode: '^1.39.0' }
```
System's node.js is >=10.0.0 so it's compatible with Azurite.

## Any outdated packages?

```dosbat
C:\>npm outdated package check
 
```

No outdated packages found. There's only one global package and it's latest' (latestlatest), so obviously up to date in this case.

## Signature verification
```dosbat
C:\Users\xxx\AppData\Roaming\npm\node_modules\azurite>npm audit signatures
audited 374 packages in 4s

374 packages have verified registry signatures

25 packages have verified attestations
(use --json --include-attestations to view attestation details)
```

No missing signatures found - which would be a sign of a malicious package.

## Running npm audit

### First pass: everything
```dosbat
C:\Users\xxx\AppData\Roaming\npm\node_modules\azurite>npm audit --no-package-lock
# npm audit report

[...]

56 vulnerabilities (10 moderate, 8 high, 38 critical)

To address issues that do not require attention, run:
  npm audit fix

To address all issues possible (including breaking changes), run:
  npm audit fix --force

Some issues need review, and may require choosing
a different dependency.
```

38 critical. That can't be so bad, after all it's a Microsoft-maintained emulator that thousands of people use daily, which means either the world is on fire, or — more likely — `npm audit` is over-counting in some ways.

There's a catch: need to filter out dev dependencies to get to what actually matters at runtime.

### Second pass: runtime only

```dosbat
C:\Users\xxx\AppData\Roaming\npm\node_modules\azurite>npm audit --no-package-lock --omit=dev
# npm audit report

[...]

10 vulnerabilities (6 moderate, 4 high)

To address issues that do not require attention, run:
  npm audit fix

To address all issues (including breaking changes), run:
  npm audit fix --force
```

#### High severity vulnerabilities

| Package | Vulnerability Summary | Fix Available |
| --- | --- | --- |
| lodash | Code Injection via `_.template`; Prototype Pollution via `_.unset`/`_.omit` | npm audit fix |
| path-to-regexp | ReDoS via multiple route parameters | npm audit fix |
| axios | 15+ issues: SSRF, CSRF, Credential Leakage, No_Proxy Bypass, Prototype Pollution, DoS, Metadata Exfiltration | npm audit fix --force |

#### Moderate severity vulnerabilities

| Package | Vulnerability Summary | Fix Available |
| --- | --- | --- |
| brace-expansion | Zero-step sequence → process hang & memory exhaustion (ReDoS) | npm audit fix |
| follow-redirects | Leaks Custom Auth Headers to Cross-Domain Redirect Targets | npm audit fix |
| `@azure/identity` | Elevation of Privilege (Azure Identity & MSAL) | npm audit fix --force |
| tough-cookie | Prototype Pollution | npm audit fix --force |
| xml2js | Prototype Pollution | npm audit fix --force |


### Understanding the blast radius

Let's take 2 examples to illustrate the use of the package dependency tree before fixing any package.

**Example 1: Simple case**
The package brace-expansion is only in 1 copy in the azurite dependency tree. The blast radius in case of vulnerabilities and fix is relatively limited.
Running a npm audit fix will likely be without issues.

```dosbat
C:\Users\xxx\AppData\Roaming\npm\node_modules\azurite>npm ls brace-expansion
azurite@3.35.0 C:\Users\xxx\AppData\Roaming\npm\node_modules\azurite
`-- rimraf@3.0.2
  `-- glob@7.2.3
    `-- minimatch@3.1.5
      `-- brace-expansion@1.1.12
```

**Example 2: Multiple copies case**

```dosbat
C:\Users\xxx\AppData\Roaming\npm\node_modules\azurite>npm ls follow-redirects
azurite@3.35.0 C:\Users\xxx\AppData\Roaming\npm\node_modules\azurite
+-- @azure/ms-rest-js@1.11.2
| `-- axios@0.21.4
|   `-- follow-redirects@1.15.11 deduped
`-- axios@0.27.2
  `-- follow-redirects@1.15.11
```

The axios package is in 2 verisons and based on the same follow-redirects package. An issue with the follow-redirects package will have a higher blast radius in case of vulnerability and fix issues. Implementation of a fix could also be more complex due to more applications being impacted.
In this case, follow-redirects is a transitive dependency can easily be updated with a npm audit fix.

### To fix or not to fix

[GitHub issue #2500](https://github.com/Azure/Azurite/issues/2500) has been tracking these vulnerabilities for a while with no ETA. The maintainers are presumably making a risk call: fix cost vs. actual exposure. Fair enough — I have to make the same call for my system.

### The fix plan

**Non-breaking fix:** covers 2/3 of the high-severity issues and 2/5 of the moderates. Low risk, clear win.

**Breaking fix (`--force`):** would tackle the rest, but risks silently breaking Azurite. My usage is purely local — with appropriate host `127.0.0.1` and port `10000`, no production data, no risky network exposure. The risk of a broken emulator outweighs the marginal security gain. I'll revisit when the maintainers ship a proper fix (Cf. set a reminder in my calendar).

### Applying the safe fixes

In my context, let's use a minimal sanity check test before and after applying the audit fix, no sandboxing — [KISS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/KISS_principle). 

**Azurite run test — successful**
```dosbat
C:\Users\xxx>azurite --blobHost 127.0.0.1 --blobPort 10000
Azurite Blob service is starting at http://127.0.0.1:10000
Azurite Blob service is successfully listening at http://127.0.0.1:10000
Azurite Queue service is starting at http://127.0.0.1:10001
Azurite Queue service is successfully listening at http://127.0.0.1:10001
Azurite Table service is starting at http://127.0.0.1:10002
Azurite Table service is successfully listening at http://127.0.0.1:10002
[CTRL-C to stop Azurite]
Azurite Blob service is closing...
Azurite Queue service is closing...
Azurite Table service is closing...
Azurite Table service successfully closed
Azurite Blob service successfully closed
Azurite Queue service successfully closed
```

**Versions of packages before audit fix**
```dosbat
C:\Users\xxx\AppData\Roaming\npm\node_modules\azurite>npm ls lodash
azurite@3.35.0 C:\Users\xxx\AppData\Roaming\npm\node_modules\azurite
`-- sequelize@6.37.8
  `-- lodash@4.17.23

[...]
```

| Package          | Version |
| ---------------- | ------- |
| lodash           | 4.17.23 |
| path-to-regexp   | 0.1.12  |
| brace-expansion  | 1.1.12  |
| follow-redirects | 1.15.11 |


**Apply audit fix**

Azurite being a global package on my system, the *npm audit fix* command cannot be used (adding a lock file as in a local Azurite package is not a good practice). So *npm install -g azurite@latest* is used instead.

```dosbat	
C:\>npm install -g azurite@latest

[npm warn deprecated messages...]

removed 5 packages, and changed 370 packages in 11s

C:\>azurite --version
3.35.0
```

**Checking non-breaking fix success**

```dosbat
C:\Users\xxx\AppData\Roaming\npm\node_modules\azurite>npm ls lodash
azurite@3.35.0 C:\Users\xxx\AppData\Roaming\npm\node_modules\azurite
`-- sequelize@6.37.8
  `-- lodash@4.18.1

[...]
```

| Package          | Version Before Update | Version After Update |
| ---------------- | --------------------- | --------------------- |
| lodash           | 4.17.23               | 4.18.1                |
| path-to-regexp   | 0.1.12                | 0.1.13                |
| brace-expansion  | 1.1.12                | 1.1.14                |
| follow-redirects | 1.15.11               | 1.16.0                |

All 4 packages bumped to patched versions, and the associated vulnerabilities disappeared from the audit report. The breaking-fix issues remain, as expected.

```dosbat
C:\Users\xxx\AppData\Roaming\npm\node_modules\azurite>npm audit --no-package-lock --omit=dev
# npm audit report

@azure/identity  <4.2.1
Severity: moderate
[...]

axios  <=0.31.0
Severity: high
[...]

tough-cookie  <4.1.3
Severity: moderate
[...]

xml2js  <0.5.0
Severity: moderate
[...]

6 vulnerabilities (4 moderate, 2 high)

To address all issues (including breaking changes), run:
  npm audit fix --force
```

**Azurite run test — successful**
```dosbat
C:\Users\xxx>azurite --blobHost 127.0.0.1 --blobPort 10000
Azurite Blob service is starting at http://127.0.0.1:10000
Azurite Blob service is successfully listening at http://127.0.0.1:10000
Azurite Queue service is starting at http://127.0.0.1:10001
Azurite Queue service is successfully listening at http://127.0.0.1:10001
Azurite Table service is starting at http://127.0.0.1:10002
Azurite Table service is successfully listening at http://127.0.0.1:10002
Azurite Blob service is closing...
Azurite Queue service is closing...
Azurite Table service is closing...
Azurite Table service successfully closed
Azurite Blob service successfully closed
Azurite Queue service successfully closed
```

# Conclusion

Node.js was behind and carrying 2 high CVEs. Azurite had a longer list of issues, some of which the non-breaking fix took care of — the remainder require changes the maintainers haven't shipped yet, and my risk profile doesn't require immediate action.

The number that surprised me most wasn't the CVE count for Azurite but the fact that its release label was the same *latest* `3.35.0` both before and after the update. The same `3.35.0` Azurite release label can in fact have different nested packages underneath. That's the kind of "false sense of security" that quietly bites you: being on the *latest release* doesn't mean you have the *latest dependencies*. Worth keeping in mind whenever you're evaluating your own npm packages supply chain exposure.
